title: Alaska Abolition Bill Author David Eastman Wins Primary
author: James Silberman
orientation: Coverage of Rep. David Eastman's August 2020 Republican primary win in Alaska HD10, framed as a referendum on abolitionism vs. incrementalism in which Alaska Right to Life backed Eastman against Alaska Family Council's preferred challenger Jesse Sumner. The article ties the result to a parallel Oklahoma SD7 race and predicts continued momentum for the Alaska Abolition of Abortion Act.

questions.question: What was the margin and significance of David Eastman's primary victory?
questions.answer: Eastman defeated Jesse Sumner roughly 52 to 48 percent in a district that is about three-quarters Republican, making his return to the Alaska State House very likely after the November general election.
questions.quote: He defeated challenger Jesse Sumner 52.24 percent to 47.76 percent. The district is about 75 percent Republican, making it very likely that he will defeat his general election opponent, Democrat Monica Stein Olsen, on November 3 and return to the Alaska State House.

questions.question: How did the endorsements split between abolitionist and incrementalist organizations?
questions.answer: Alaska Right to Life, an abolition-only organization unaffiliated with National Right to Life, backed Eastman, while Alaska Family Council, which only supports incremental pro-life bills, backed Sumner.
questions.quote: Eastman was endorsed by Alaska Right to Life which, despite the name, is not affiliated with National Right to Life. Alaska Right to Life is an organization which only supports abolition bills. Sumner was endorsed by the Alaska Family Council, an incrementalist organization that only supports incremental pro-life bills and does not support the Alaska Abolition of Abortion Act.

questions.question: How did establishment PAC money factor into the race?
questions.answer: Sumner was supported by establishment PACs with large budgets, including one that also funds pro-abortion Republican Senator Lisa Murkowski, which spent tens of thousands attacking Eastman, but the abolitionist still prevailed.
questions.quote: Sumner was supported by establishment PACs with huge budgets. One of the same PACs that funds the campaigns of pro-abortion Republican US Senator Lisa Murkowski spent tens of thousands against Eastman. Still, Eastman, the abolitionist, came out on top.

questions.question: How does Alaska Right to Life characterize the difference between their position and incrementalism?
questions.answer: Pat Martin, director of Alaska Right to Life, frames the disagreement not as a strategic choice but as a moral and biblical one, calling deviation from protecting every innocent human life a perversion of justice rather than a different strategy.
questions.quote: And we must never forget that deviating from God's command to protect every innocent human life isn't just a "different strategy," but a perversion of justice.

questions.question: What is Matt Johnson's critique of incrementalist organizations like Alaska Family Action?
questions.answer: Johnson argues in Restoring Liberty that incrementalist groups are unwittingly being manipulated by the Republican establishment, which prefers nibbling at Roe because it provides political cover without requiring courageous votes.
questions.quote: Alaska Family Action wants to pursue an incremental strategy of nibbling away at the edges of Roe v. Wade and the abortion industry. The Republican Establishment, of course, loves this idea. It gives them cover. They can continue to pay lip-service at election time, and they don't really have to vote on legislation that requires much courage or risk on their part.

questions.question: How does the article connect the Alaska race to Oklahoma's SD7 contest?
questions.answer: The article draws parallels between Eastman vs. Sumner and the Warren Hamilton vs. Larry Boggs race in Oklahoma SD7, noting that both featured abolitionists facing incrementalist-backed opponents bankrolled by establishment PACs, with abolitionists winning in both cases.
questions.quote: There were two big similarities between this race and the OK SD7 race between abolitionist Warren Hamilton and incrementalist Larry Boggs.

questions.question: What other abolitionist candidate succeeded alongside Eastman?
questions.answer: Christopher Kurka, the former director of Alaska Right to Life and a supporter of the Alaska Abolition of Abortion Act, won his first race for HD7 with more than 71 percent of the vote.
questions.quote: Christopher Kurka, the candidate referred to in Johnson's article, ran for office for the first time and handily defeated his Republican opponent with more than 71 percent of the vote in the AK HD7 race. Kurka is the former director of Alaska Right to Life and a supporter of the Alaska Abolition of Abortion Act.

questions.question: What does the article predict for abolition legislation in Alaska going forward?
questions.answer: With Eastman and Kurka headed to the State House and most prior abolition-bill supporters surviving the primary, the article predicts that an abolition bill will almost certainly be filed the next year with vocal supporters.
questions.quote: With Eastman and Kurka well on their way to the State House, there almost certainly will be an abolition bill in Alaska next year and it will have vocal supporters.
